coredata.dc:title: Molecular aggregation-dependent “turn-on” fluorescence strategy: Carbon quantum dots as sensing material for dual-mode detection of intact TATP at nanomolar level

coredata.dc:description: One of the most important problems of peroxide-based explosives is their determination in intact form without preliminary conversion to H2O2. We have overcome this difficulty by developing an innovative approach for directly determining intact triacetone triperoxide (TATP) for the first time. Herein, nitrogen- and oxygen-doped carbon quantum dots (APT-CDs; Ascorbic acid-Polyethyleneimine-Triethylenetetramine Carbon quantum Dots) with high quantum yield (66.8 %) were synthesized via microwave-assisted method. The fluorescence of APT-CDs, which exhibits blue-fluorescence, is quenched in the presence of acetone by photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and hydrogen-bonding mechanisms. By adding intact TATP in the critical concentration range to an acetone solution of APT-CDs at quenched fluorescence, a significant portion of the initial fluorescence is recovered. The recovered fluorescence can be monitored both by spectrofluorometry and smartphone-application. TATP forms molecular aggregates as a result of noncovalent interactions with acetone molecules that interact (via H-bonding) with APT-CDs at ground-state, thereby interrupting the interaction of a certain part of acetone molecules with carbon dots. This method provides a TATP detection limit (LOD) of 0.18 nmol l -1 (0.75 nmol l -1 for smartphone-application). TATP determination occurs with high recoveries (94.2 %–105.7 %), and possible interferents do not have a significant effect on TATP determination.

head.ce:title: Molecular aggregation-dependent “turn-on” fluorescence strategy: Carbon quantum dots as sensing material for dual-mode detection of intact TATP at nanomolar level

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: One of the most important problems of peroxide-based explosives is their determination in intact form without preliminary conversion to H2O2. We have overcome this difficulty by developing an innovative approach for directly determining intact triacetone triperoxide (TATP) for the first time. Herein, nitrogen- and oxygen-doped carbon quantum dots (APT-CDs; Ascorbic acid-Polyethyleneimine-Triethylenetetramine Carbon quantum Dots) with high quantum yield (66.8 %) were synthesized via microwave-assisted method. The fluorescence of APT-CDs, which exhibits blue-fluorescence, is quenched in the presence of acetone by photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and hydrogen-bonding mechanisms. By adding intact TATP in the critical concentration range to an acetone solution of APT-CDs at quenched fluorescence, a significant portion of the initial fluorescence is recovered. The recovered fluorescence can be monitored both by spectrofluorometry and smartphone-application. TATP forms molecular aggregates as a result of noncovalent interactions with acetone molecules that interact (via H-bonding) with APT-CDs at ground-state, thereby interrupting the interaction of a certain part of acetone molecules with carbon dots. This method provides a TATP detection limit (LOD) of 0.18 nmol l -1 (0.75 nmol l -1 for smartphone-application). TATP determination occurs with high recoveries (94.2 %–105.7 %), and possible interferents do not have a significant effect on TATP determination.

The blue fluorescence of APT-CDs is quenched by PET and H-bonding mechanisms in acetone solvent. At this stage, as a result of the addition of TATP to the medium, TATP forms molecular aggregates with acetone molecules involving solute-solvent interactions and the H-bonds of acetone molecules with APT-CDs are broken. Thus, the fluorescence of APT-CDs is significantly recovered depending on the TATP concentration.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Triacetone triperoxide (TATP), an organic peroxide-based explosive, was first discovered by Wolffenstein in the 19th century [1]. TATP can be easily synthesized from hydrogen peroxide and acetone using hydrochloric or sulfuric acid as catalyst [2]. TATP, which is not used for industrial or military purposes due to its unstable nature, is frequently used by terrorist groups due to its readily available starting materials (e.g., hydrogen peroxide from hair dyes and antiseptics and acetone from nail polish removers) [3–5]. The peroxide-based explosives triacetone triperoxide (TATP) and hexamethylene triperoxide diamine (HMTD) were first seized by Israeli police in the late 1970s [6–7]. Among the terrorist actions carried out using TATP, the London underground attack (2005), in which many people were injured and killed, stands out [6]. Since similar attacks have repercussions all over the world, the identification of organic peroxide-based explosives has become important [8–9].

TATP, which has a white crystal structure, is difficult to determine by spectroscopic methods due to its easy sublimation and lack of functional groups such as nitro (-NO2) in its structure [10]. In indirect TATP determinations carried out by spectroscopic methods, H2O2, which is formed as a result of the acidic hydrolysis of TATP, is used [11]. Apart from spectroscopic methods, various analytical methods have been developed for the determination of TATP, such as liquid chromatography (LC)[12], mass spectrometry coupled to gas chromatography (GC–MS)[13] or liquid chromatography (LC-MS)[14]. However, these complex techniques are relatively expensive, time-consuming, and require various solvents [15]. As an alternative to these methods, simple-to-apply and easily accessible sensors based on carbon quantum dots can be preferred.

Zero-dimensional (0D) carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which belong to the functional nanomaterial class of quantum dots that received the Nobel prize in chemistry in 2023, have been widely used in many fields due to their superior properties such as simple fabrication processes, tunable fluorescence properties, high biocompatibility, and low toxicity. Carbon quantum dots were accidentally discovered by Xu et al. in 2004 during the separation and purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) [16]. This discovery triggered subsequent studies to utilize the fluorescent properties of CQDs and create a new class of applicable fluorescent nanomaterials. Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles were named “carbon quantum dots” by Sun et al. in 2006, who proposed a synthetic route to produce CQDs with much higher fluorescence emission via surface passivation [17]. CQDs are typically hemispherical graphene/graphene oxide layered nanoparticles consisting of an amorphous and nanocrystalline core with dominant graphitic sp2-hybridized carbon and heteroatom-bonded sp3-hybridized carbon groups fused together. CQDs contain significant amounts of heteroatoms due to surface passivation [18–22]. These heteroatoms provide carbon quantum dots with excellent dispersion properties in water and enable many inorganic and organic analytes to interact with CQDs. Increasing surface passivation also increases the fluorescence properties of CQDs [23]. In addition, the variety and amount of functional groups also change the fluorescence properties of CQDs in aqueous and nonaqueous solutions.

In this study, a fluorometric nanoprobe based on carbon quantum dots was developed for the fast, simple, and sensitive determination of TATP. This nanoprobe can exhibit different fluorescence properties in different solvent media. Additionally, its fluorescence is completely quenched in acetone solvent. When TATP in the critical concentration range is added to the nanoprobe in acetone medium, the nanoprobe is partially released as a result of aggregation of TATP molecules in acetone. This results in the recovery of the quenched fluorescence of the nanoprobe in the presence of TATP, forming the essence of the developed detection method.

L-ascorbic acid (AA), polyethylenimine (PEI), triethylenetetramine (TETA), acetone, and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), were purchased from Sigma (St. Louis, Missouri, USA). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethanol (EtOH), methanol (MeOH), tetrahydrofuran (THF), dimethylformamide (DMF), metal salts (CaCl2, ZnCl2, NaCl, MnCl2, FeCl2, CuCl2, MgCl2, CdCl2, NaNO3, NaNO2, Na2SO4, and Na2CO3) were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). All chemicals used were of analytical reagent grade.

The explosive substances 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP, picric acid; pure), 1,3,5,7-tetranitro-1,3,5,7-tetrazocane (HMX; pure), 1,3,5-trinitroperhydro-1,3,5-triazine (RDX; which contains 85 % active compound), 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT; pure), pentaerythritol tetranitrate (PETN; pure), and 2,4,6-trinitrophenylmethylnitramine (TETRYL; pure) were kindly supplied by the Mechanical and Chemical Industry Corporation (Makine Kimya Endüstrisi Kurumu–MKEK; Ankara, Turkey) from previous projects. RTC blended clean sandy soil, used as a sample matrix for the determination of TATP explosive, was obtained from Sigma–Aldrich (St. Louis, Missouri, USA).
All aqueous-solutions used in the experiments were prepared with ultrapure-water (18.2 MΩ·cm, Millipore).

Fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of APT-CDs were recorded by a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA) with using Suprasil quartz cuvette (Hellma Analytics, USA). Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra of APT-CDs were measured by a UV–1900i UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with using Suprasil quartz cuvettes (Hellma Analytics, USA). A Quattro FEG-ESEM (FEI, USA) scanning transmission electron microscope was used to investigate the morphologies of APT-CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the APT-CDs was analyzed through a K–Alpha spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). The infrared spectra were obtained on an Alpha-T spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany) in the 4000–400 cm−1 range with the KBr pellet technique. The Raman spectrum at excitation wavelength of 532 nm was recorded on a INVIA Microscopic Confocal Raman Spectrometer (Renishaw, England) equipped with He-Ne laser. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiments were performed on a 90Plus particle size analyzer (Brookhaven Instrument, USA) with using a 35 mW HeNe laser at a temperature of 25.0 ± 0.2 °C in water.

Milestone ETHOS-ONE (Shelton, CT, USA) microwave extraction system was used for synthesis of the APT-CDs. The magnetic stirring, centrifuge, and ultrasonic bath of the solutions was carried out on a HSM 250D heating mantles with magnetic stirring (Thermomac, Germany), a Hettich Universal 320 centrifuge (Germany), and a WiseClean ultrasonic bath (Wisd, Germany), respectively. Drying processes for obtained APT-CDs was carried out a WiseVen (Wisd, Germany) vacuum oven. A LC–20A liquid chromatograph (Shimadzu, Japan) coupled with a LCMS–8040 mass spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was used for the proposed method validation. LC was equipped with a Ultra–AQ column (Restek, USA – 100 × 2.1 mm, 3 μm, C18).

TATP was synthesized using the acid-catalyzed method developed in the literature[2]. As shown in Scheme S1, acetone (0.47 mL), hydrogen peroxide (0.68 mL, 30 %), and sulfuric acid (conc., 2 drops), which acts as a catalyst, were added respectively to the beaker in the 0 °C ice bath in the fume hood. TATP was synthesized as a white solid by keeping the prepared mixture at room temperature and in an acetone atmosphere for 24 h. To remove unreacted species, the synthesized solid was washed with 25.0 mL of ultrapure water-acetone solvent mixture (7:3, v/v) and filtered with a vacuum pump. Pure white TATP crystals were placed in a glass vial and stored in a refrigerator and acetone environment.

Safety Note: TATP is an extremely dangerous material, which may lead to explosions under impact, friction, and temperature changes. Its synthesis may only be carried out by highly qualified personnel and at small quantities not exceeding 100 mg (as higher amounts may cause spontaneous explosions) [12].

APT-CDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted method[24] under the amounts and reaction conditions given in Table S1. Briefly, l-ascorbic acid (AA), polyethylenimine (PEI), and triethylenetetramine (TETA) were dissolved ultra pure water (20 mL) in a teflon reactor. Then, the teflon reactor was placed in the microwave and kept in the microwave under different reaction conditions (Fig. S1, Reaction Conditions: the temperature was increased to 180 °C for 0–5 min. Then the temperature was kept constant at 180 °C for 5–20 min, and the temperature was reduced from 180 °C to 80 °C for 20–30 min. During all these processes, the power of the microwave device was set to 800 W.) At the end of the period, the brown mixture was taken from the reactor cooled to room temperature and placed on the dialysis bag (MWCO 3500). The mixture was dialyzed against ultrapure water at room temperature for 36 h. The mixture taken from the dialysis membrane was dried in a vacuum oven and pure APT-CDs, which gave off strong blue fluorescence, were obtained.

The quantum yield (QY) of APT-CDs was measured by comparing the fluorescence intensities and absorption values of APT-CDs samples with anthracene (quantum yield 0.27, dissolved in ethanol, excitation wavelength: 360 nm). In order to minimize re-absorption effect, the absorbance of APT-CDs samples was kept below 0.1. The quantum yield of APT-CDs was measured according to:
(1)

where ϕ was the quantum yield, I was the measured integrated emission intensity, A was the absorbance measured on the ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer and η is the refractive index. The subscript “S” and “R” refer to APT-CDs and reference standard substance (the refractive index of water was 1.33 and of alcohol was 1.36), respectively. The quantum yield of APT-CDs found by equation-1 was 66.8 %. All obtained parameters are summarized in Table S2.
The preparation of each solution used throughout the experiments is described in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of APT-CDs for TATP
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitive detection of TATP was made with a spectrofluorometer. Firstly, a stock solution of APT-CDs (0.01 g mL-1) was prepared in acetone. Then APT-CDs solution (250 µL) and different amounts of TATP solution (5 µL to 60 µL from 4.5 × 10–7 mol l -1) were added to a series of Eppendorf tubes. The final volumes of the solutions were made up to 2.0 mL with acetone. The solution was allowed to stand for 10 min to enable complete interactions. The fluorescence measurements were carried out between the emission range of 400 nm to 600 nm (λex= 355 nm). All experimental trials were performed at room temperature.

Selectivity study for the determination of TATP was performed in the presence of different types of explosives materials (HMTD, NTO, TNT, TETRYL, RDX, HMX, TNP, and PETN) and hydrolyzed of TATP and HMTD explosives (H2O2). Accordingly, APT-CDs solution (250 µL, 0.01 g mL-1) was added to a series of Eppendorf tubes. Afterwards, the test solutions (50 µL, 4.5 × 10–7 mol l -1, TATP, HMTD, H2O2, NTO, TNT, TETRYL, RDX, HMX, TNP, and PETN) were added separately to the Eppendorf tubes. The final volumes of the mixtures were made up to 2.0 mL with acetone. After allowing 10 min to enable complete interactions. The fluorescence emission spectra of all prepared solutions were recorded in the wavelength range of 400 nm to 600 nm (λex= 355 nm).

The accuracy of TATP determination in the presence of possible interferences such as selected metal cations (Fe2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, and Mg2+), common anions (Cl-, SO4 2-, CO3 2-, NO3 -, and NO2 -), explosives (HMTD, NTO, TNT, TETRYL, RDX, HMX, TNP, and PETN), hydrolyzed (H2O2) and camouflage materials (detergent, glucose, aspartame, paracetamol, and acetylsalicylic acid) was investigated. Potential interferents (metal cations and anions at 10-fold; 50 µL; 4.5 × 10–6 mol l -1, explosives at 5-fold; 50 µL; 2.25 × 10–6 mol l -1, and camouflage materials at 20-fold; 50 µL; 9.0 × 10–6 mol l -1) were added to vials containing stock APT-CDs (250 µL; 0.01 g mL-1) and TATP (50 µL; 4.5 × 10–7 mol l -1) was added to each Eppendorf tubes. Final volumes of the solutions were made up to 2.0 mL with acetone. Fluorescence emission spectra of the prepared solutions were recorded in the wavelength range of 400 nm to 600 nm (λex= 355 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of soil extracts for real-sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: As a certified reference standard, 1.0 g of clean sandy soil (uncontaminated soil #1, CLN SOIL-1) was weighed and transferred to a beaker. The sandy soil in the beaker was artificially contaminated with a 2.5 mL solution of TATP at different concentration (1.0 × 10–3, 4.5 × 10–3, and 8.0 × 10–3 mol l -1) prepared in acetone solvent. The soil sample contaminated with TATP was homogenized in an ultrasonic bath for 5 min and dried in a vacuum oven at 50 °C. For back-extraction of TATP from sandy soil, the dried soil sample was washed with 2.0 mL of acetone and the heterogeneous solution was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 5 min). The supernatant was filtered (CHROMOFIL PET-20/25) into a 25.0 mL flask and the final volume of the solution was made up to 25.0 mL with acetone. This process was repeated three-times for each clean sandy soil sample. The TATP extract solution was diluted 100-times and TATP was determined by the recommended procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reference chromatographic method for validation of TATP detection
ce:section.ce:para: The literature method involving gas chromatography with mass spectrometry (GC–MS) was applied to validate the proposed TATP detection method [25]. All experimental details of the reference GC–MS assay are as described in the Supporting Information.

Fluorescence experiments are usually performed in a laboratory setting using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. However, this process is not suitable for on-site detection. In the developed method, the synthesized APT-CDs have high photostability and their fluorescence, which is quenched in the acetone medium, is restored with the addition of TATP. With this theory, color analyses of the APT-CDs solution, which had no fluorescence in the solution medium initially (in the absence of TATP) and did not show any blue fluorescence, and the solutions that showed an increasing amount of bright blue fluorescence after the addition of TATP, were performed on-site using a smartphone software (Colorimeter application for Android-OS phones, Version 1.6.6.6). To obtain stable results, a 365 nm excited UV-lamp was integrated in a dark-box. The sample cuvettes placed in the portable dark-box were placed under UV-light and 5 cm away from the phone camera. In this way, the phone camera was able to focus accurately every time and perform color analysis of the fluorescence seen under UV-light. In the analyses made with the bright blue fluorescence observed at increasing TATP concentration, increasing fluorescence spectra were seen in the blue color region in the smartphone application. Finally, the smartphone app was used to convert the fluorescent photographs into specific red (R), green (G), and blue (B) brightness values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, morphological and structural characterization of APT-CDs as a sensing material
ce:section.ce:para: Selection of O- and N-precursors of the CDs used in this study was made according to the theoretical analysis of structure-activity relationships of carbon dots in a comprehensive review [26]. Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized that showed red shift in emission with increasing amine groups on GQDs[27] through the formation of inter-band charge-transfer states between the valence and conduction bands of CDs via hybridization of C–N atoms at the edges [28]. The type and concentration of N-centers, placed either directly within the carbon lattice or at the N–CD surface, can modify the absorption and emission, which can extend from UV to near-IR spectral range, and improve the quantum yields when compared to nitrogen-free CDs [29]. We prepared our CDs with the use of two distinct amines as σ-donators to increase the possibility of inter-band states. The use of multi-amines was aimed to increase the possibility of interaction between the solvent molecules and N-containing edge functionalities of CD[30] so as to enhance the sensitivity of the selected analytical system to acetone. The O-rich precursor ascorbic acid (AA) was preferred to the more frequently used citric acid to retard amide formation between the carboxylic groups of citric acid with the amino groups[31] of N-precursors that might decrease the alternatives of inter-band states. An additional advantage in using AA as the O-source (with 54.5 % O-content) is that AA generally causes the manufacture of spherical carbon dots with a relatively uniform size distribution [32].

For nanoprobe synthesis, APT-CDs were obtained by microwave-assisted method[21] using L-ascorbic acid as the carbon source, and polyethyleneimine and triethylenetetramine as the nitrogen sources (see the supporting information section for detailed information). Considering the synthesis steps summarized in Fig. 1 a, nine-different APT-CDs were synthesized under different reaction conditions (reaction temperature and time) with different amounts of carbon- and nitrogen-sources given in Table S1. The quantum yield of each APT-CDs was calculated and the APT-CDs with the best quantum yield were (APT-CDs_5, QY: 66.8 %); it was obtained by the reaction using 1.0 g L-ascorbic acid, 0.1 g polyethylenimine, and 0.1 g triethylenetetramine at 180 °C and in 30 min. The quantum yield of each APT-CDs was calculated using anthracene dissolved in ethanol as the reference material and with the help of the formula given in equation-1. The numerical values ​​of the parameters required for the quantum efficiency calculation of APT-CDs (APT-CDs-5) are summarized in Table S2.

The structural and surface characterization of APT-CDs with high quantum yield was carried out by STEM, DLS, IR, Raman, XRD, and XPS techniques. When the STEM image given in Fig. 1b is examined, it is seen that APT-CDs are well distributed and their structures are spherical. In addition, it is understood that the particle sizes are close to each other and in the range of 2–4 nm. In order to verify the particle sizes obtained from STEM, the particle size distribution graph of APT-CDs in solution medium was created by DLS technique. According to the graph given in Fig. 1c, it is understood that the particle sizes of APT-CDs are in the range of 2–5 nm and the average particle size is 3 ± 0.2 nm. In fact, STEM and DLS results support each other.

The XRD pattern of APT-CDs (Fig. 1d) revealed a single broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 26° that corresponded to the crystal lattice distance of (0 0 2) (d 002 = 0.34 nm), and demonstrated a well-ordered graphitic-carbon structure with layered regularity [33]. Additionally, the minor peak observed at 2θ = 39.1° (1 0 0) also supports the carbon layer.

In order to determine the functionalized groups on the graphite-layer surface, the infrared spectrum of APT-CDs was recorded (Fig. 1e). The two peaks observed at 3558 cm-1 and 3255 cm-1 in the spectrum belong to the stretching vibrations of -NH and -OH groups, respectively [34]. In addition, the double peak observed at 1411 cm-1 and 1332 cm-1 belonging to the bending vibrations of -NH[35] group, the peak observed at 1155 cm-1 belonging to the CN[35] stretching vibration, and the peak observed at 1080 cm-1 belonging to the CO[36] stretching vibration confirm that -NH and -OH groups are functionalized on the APT-CDs surface. In addition, the characteristic stretching peak of the C = O group[37] at 1698 cm-1 indicates the presence of carboxylic acid on the nanoprobe surface, while the CC peak at 1589 cm-1 supports the graphitic-structure.

APT-CDs are nanomaterials with both a graphitic core, and functionalized groups on the surface. In addition to functional group verification, the graphitic structure and graphitization degree should also be verified. In this direction, the Raman spectrum of APT-CDs was recorded (Fig. 1f). Two peaks are observed at 1337 cm-1 and 1628 cm-1 in the Raman spectrum, and these peaks represent the d- and G-bands, respectively. The d-scattering band explains the graphitic vacancy defects of sp3-hybrid amorphous carbon, while the G-scattering band explains the graphene layer of sp2-hybrid carbon [38]. In addition, the intensity ratio of the d-/G-bands of the defective carbon atoms (ID/IG) provides information about the graphitization extent. The ID/IG ratio for APT-CDs is 1.24, which confirms the high defects on the surface of APT-CDs [39].

The elemental analysis and surface composition of the obtained APT-CDs were elucidated by XPS technique. There are 3-characteristic peaks in the XPS survey spectrum given in Fig. 1 g These peaks can be attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s corresponding to 288, 405 and 534 eV, respectively. In addition, the elemental content of APT-CDs includes C, N, and O elements at 70 %, 11 %, and 15 %, respectively. When the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s is examined, the peaks of C
C, CN, CO, and CN bonds corresponding to 284, 286, 288, and 289 eV binding energies, respectively, are located (Fig. 1h). Three peaks are observed in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s shown in Fig. 1i. These peaks can be attributed to OC (530 eV), OH (531 eV), and OC (538 eV) bonds [40]. Finally, in the N1s spectrum, NH peaks are at 399 eV, NC/NC peaks are at 400 eV, and NO peaks are at 406 eV [41]. All the results obtained from XPS studies confirm that the surface of the prepared APT-CDs is functionalized by high amounts of oxygen- and nitrogen-groups. In a similar study, double-amine usage on graphene oxide was shown to increase the probability of variety and abundance of nitrogenated groups[42] on carbonaceous nano-platforms.

The optical properties of APT-CDs were investigated by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 2 a shows the absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of APT-CDs. There are two peaks in the absorption spectrum, the first of which is observed at a wavelength of 251 nm and is attributed to π-π* transitions that arise from the core of the carbon dots not held responsible for fluorescence [43]. The other peak is observed at a wavelength of 356 nm and is attributed to η-π* transitions that arise from surface defects associated with fluorescence emission [44]. The Fig. also shows the excitation spectrum of APT-CDs with a peak at 355 nm and the emission spectrum with a peak at 440 nm. The excitation spectrum and absorption spectrum of APT-CDs completely overlap. Fig. 2a-inset shows the images of APT-CDs that are pale yellow in daylight and have bright blue fluorescence under UV-lamp (λex=365 nm).

Fig. 2b shows the emission spectra obtained at different excitation wavelengths. When the excitation wavelength is increased from 310 nm to 355 nm, the intensity of the emission spectrum centered at 440 nm gradually increases. However, as the excitation wavelength is increased from 355 nm to 410 nm, the intensity of the emission spectra obtained decreases, and the center of the spectrum shifts to 460 nm. This observed red-shift indicates the low wavelength dependence of APT-CDs.

In order to see the solvent effect on the fluorescence properties of APT-CDs, the emission intensities of APT-CDs solutions in different organic solvents and 1:1 water mixtures were recorded at 440 nm wavelength (Fig. 2c). The highest fluorescence intensity of APT-CDs was observed in aqueous-medium, while the lowest fluorescence intensity was observed in acetone medium. Since the developed method was based on the “fluorescence-turn-on” mechanism, experimental studies were carried out in acetone solvent.

Photostability experiments of APT-CDs were carried out in the dark and under a UV-lamp. Fig. 2d shows the emission intensities of APT-CDs solution recorded at 440 nm wavelength every five days. After each measurement, APT-CDs solution was placed in a closed vial and stored in the refrigerator at 5 °C. APT-CDs, which showed high photostability in solution-medium for up to 40 days, also exhibited the same performance on paper. “APT-CDs” was handwritten on the paper and this paper was photographed under UV-lamp (λex=365 nm) on the first day and 40th day (Fig. 2d-inset). In both photographs, it is seen that APT-CDs text emits similar fluorescence light. Then, the prepared APT-CDs solution was kept under a UV-lamp (λex=365 nm) for 240 min and the emission intensity was recorded at 440 nm wavelength every 30 min (Fig. 2e). At the end of 3 h, it was observed that the fluorescence intensity of APT-CDs decreased by 13.67 % and this value remained constant thereafter.

Ionic strength is one of the important factors to evaluate the photostability of APT-CDs. Therefore, the effect of inert electrolyte concentration on the fluorescence intensity was evaluated in NaCl-medium (0.2 mol l -1 to 0.6 mol l -1). As seen in Fig. 2f, the fluorescence intensity of APT-CDs did not show any significant change with increasing NaCl concentration.

In the developed method, our fluorescence nanoprobe APT-CDs exhibits good fluorescence in aqueous medium. However, the fluorescence of APT-CDs prepared in acetone solvent is quenched. At this point, a certain part of the fluorescence of APT-CDs is recovered by adding TATP prepared in acetone to the medium (Fig. S2). In the method designed for this special case, it was examined in detail whether a similar effect could be observed with APT-CDs and/or TATP prepared in different solvents and also whether TATP has any critical concentration range, and the necessary parameters were optimized (Fig. S3). In the experiments performed, it was seen that the turn-on fluorescence mechanism of APT-CDs and TATP only works in acetone medium and at nanomolar level concentrations of TATP.
Optimization of the critical parameters for the method and the Figures (Figs. S2 and S3) related to the optimization are described in detail in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of fluorescence performance of APT-CDs
Selectivity for the detection of TATP
ce:section.ce:para: In practical application, the presence of other explosives can potentially interfere with the TATP detection process. As seen in Fig. 3 a, selectivity studies were performed in the presence of different energetics (TATP, HMTD, NTO, TNT, TETRYL, RDX, HMX, TNP, PETN, and TATP acidic degradation product: H2O2). When the emission spectra and bar histograms (Fig. 3b) at 440 nm are examined, it is seen that the fluorescence of APT-CDs, whose fluorescence is quenched in acetone, is recovered in the presence of TATP and APT-CDs exhibit high selectivity to TATP. Apart from TATP, HMTD (another peroxide-based energetic substance) responds. However, this signal is approximately one fourth of the signal obtained in the presence of TATP. In addition, in the photographs taken under UV-lamp (λex=365 nm) given in Fig. 3b-inset, fluorescence is observed only in the presence of TATP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity for the detection of TATP
ce:section.ce:para:
To elucidate the TATP detection sensitivity of APT-CDs, TATP prepared in acetone (1.125 to 13.50 nmol l -1 at final conc.) was added to a series of APT-CDs solutions. In the obtained emission spectra, the emission intensity of APT-CDs at 440 nm wavelength increases proportionally with increasing TATP concentration (Fig. 4 a). TATP concentration of 13.50 nmol l -1 is the limit for the fluorescence increase of APT-CDs and values ​​above this concentration do not allow further fluorescence increase (detailed explanations are given in the Plausible Mechanism for the “Turn-On” Fluorescence of APT-CDs section). A calibration curve was created by plotting the emission intensities obtained at 440 nm wavelength against TATP concentration (Fig. 4b). In addition, there is a linear relationship between TATP concentration and emission intensities. The equation of the curve is given below.

where I represents the fluorescence intensity of TATP interacting with APT-CDs, and C represents the final nanomolar concentration (in nmol l -1) of TATP. The limit of detection (LOD) for TATP was calculated as 0.18 nmol l -1 (LOD = 3σbl/m, σbl standing for the standard deviation of the blank emission intensity at 440 nm, and m the slope of the linear portion of the calibration curve), and the coefficients of variation were 0.97 % (within-day) and 1.12 % (between-day).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible mechanism for the “Turn-On” fluorescence of APT-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this method developed for the direct determination of TATP, the “turn-on” fluorescence mechanism can be explained by firstly investigating the interaction between APT-CDs and acetone. At this point, the APT-CDs solution was prepared with increasing contents of acetone from full aqueous-medium to full acetone-medium and the emission spectra of the prepared solutions were recorded. The recorded emission spectra showed that increasing acetone ratios in the solution medium quenched the fluorescence of APT-CDs (Fig. S4a). In addition, this quenching was directly proportional to the acetone concentration (Fig. S4b). This linear relationship observed in the presence of acetone was examined in more detail and Stern-Volmer (Fig. S5a) and Benesi-Hildebrand (Fig. S5b) plots were created. In the Stern-Volmer plot, a linear relationship was obtained up to a certain concentration of acetone, while a curve was obtained at high concentrations. This shows that acetone works with both dynamic and static mechanisms in quenching the fluorescence of APT-CDs [45]. The association constant (Ka) obtained from the Benesi-Hildebrand plot was calculated as 1.3 × 102 M-1. The magnitude of the equilibrium constant shows that there is a weak ground-state interaction between APT-CDs and acetone. In the carbon nanodots-based fluorometric acetone determination method developed by Sai et al. in 2020, it was reported that the fluorescence quenching observed in the presence of acetone proceeds with a dynamic mechanism based on PET and a static mechanism based on ground-state interaction (H-bonding) [37]. The H-bonding observed at the ground-state is between the carbonyl oxygen of acetone (R,R’-C = O) and the hydrogen atoms of the amine groups doped on the nanodot surface (CDots-NH2). However, this interaction is weak and assists the dynamic mechanism. To verify this situation, APT-CDs solution was prepared in 2-butanone (methyl ethyl ketone) solvent medium, which has a ketone structure like acetone, and the emission spectrum of this solution in Fig. S6 shows that butanone quenches the fluorescence of APT-CDs but not as strong as acetone. The carbonyl oxygen of butanone solvent also forms H-bonds (though at a weaker level than of acetone) with the amine hydrogens doped on the APT-CDs surface (R,R’-C=O…H2N…CDots), thus quenching the fluorescence of APT-CDs [46]. However, butanone does not interact with APT-CDs in the excited-state.

In order to confirm the dominant dynamic-quenching mechanism besides the static-mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime spectrum of APT-CDs with and without acetone interaction was recorded (Fig. S7). In the spectrum, the fluorescence lifetime of APT-CDs is 9.68±0.4 ns, but this period decreases to 6.01±0.3 ns in the presence of acetone, and the two spectral curves are different from each other. The dominant dynamic-quenching mechanism observed is the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism [47]. However, as is known, PET is of two types, i.e. acceptor-PET and donor-PET [48]. In order to determine the type of PET mechanism observed in our method, zeta potential measurement of APT-CDs was performed (Fig. S8). The surface charge of APT-CDs was found to be −25.46 mV. The calculated energy level diagrams revealed that the excited π* orbital of APT-CDs lies above the LUMO orbital of acetone and electron transfer from APT-CDs to the empty orbital of acetone occurs by the mechanism of donor-PET. These analyses and calculations showed that the fluorescence quenching observed for APT-CDs in the presence of acetone occurs by the dominant donor-PET mechanism supported by H-bonding interaction.

When increasing amounts of TATP (prepared in acetone) are added to the APT-CDs solution whose fluorescence was quenched in the presence of acetone, a certain part of the fluorescence of APT-CDs is recovered. As mentioned in the optimization studies, the fluorescence of APT-CDs is quenched by 93.67 % with acetone and in this case, 30.2 % of the quenched fluorescence is recovered by adding TATP to the APT-CDs solution. This recovery does not change at high TATP concentrations. The main reason behind the “turn-on” fluorescence mechanism observed in the presence of TATP is most likely the formation of molecular aggregates of TATP in acetone. It can be reasoned that the TATP molecules added in the critical concentration range tend to cluster and form aggregates as a result of non-covalent interactions with acetone molecules [49,50]. In the literature, it was hypothesized that aggregation in non-aqueous media is a complicated process involving hydrophobic-lipophilic interactions arising from aggregation and self-coiling of organic molecules. Solute-solvent interactions may give rise to noncovalent supramolecular aggregates [51,52]. In order to confirm this phenomenon, DLS spectra of APT-CDs without and with TATP (in acetone medium) were recorded. In the DLS spectrum of APT-CDs given in Fig. S9a, a peak with an average particle size of 4.1 ± 0.4 nm is observed with particle sizes ranging from 2 to 10 nm. In the DLS spectrum of the APT-CDs solution with TATP, the major peak (average particle size of 4.5 ± 0.4 nm) remains constant, but another peak centered at 28±2 nm is observed (Fig. S9b). In order to examine this situation supporting our theory in more detail, STEM images of both solutions (APT-CDs and APT-CDs+TATP) were recorded. In the STEM image of APT-CDs, spherical particles with sizes ranging from 3 to 5 nm and well dispersed in the solution medium are clearly seen (Fig. S10a). In the STEM image of APT-CDs with TATP added, APT-CDs particles with sizes ranging from 3 to 5 nm are visualized, and in addition to these particles, aggregates with sizes of 22–25 nm are also seen (Fig. S10b). Additionally, APT-CDs appear to localize around molecular aggregates as well as being dispersed in solution. Naturally, it is possible that these aggregates formed around the original nanodots (APT-CDs) in a way functioning as nuclei for further clustering. There may also be local solubility differences of TATP molecules around the probing CDs in acetone solution. Previous experimental studies of mixtures of acetone and water by other researchers indicate that water molecules tend to self-aggregate in acetonated solutions [53]. Since even HPLC-grade acetone may contain minute amounts of water, wet (hydrated) triacetone triperoxide (TATP•H2O and TATP•2H2O) clusters have been observed by using broadband rotational spectroscopy[54]; these clusters may also be responsible for the solubility differences in our study leading to nano-aggregate formation mechanism forming the initial step of fluorescence recovery.

For the sake of considering another possibility, the CDs’ surface amine groups are expected to act as H-bond donors. In that case, TATP by itself could only be a H-bond acceptor alternative to acetone (as opposed to the simple peroxide H2O2 which can act as both H-bond donor and H-bond acceptor). In fact, TATP cannot behave as a hydrogen-bond donor because its structural formula (-C(CH3)2-(OO)-)3 is devoid of H-atoms bound to electronegative atoms. On the other hand, acetone was proven to be a polar aprotic hydrogen bond-acceptor solvent [55]. Acetone is at a much higher concentration in the medium than TATP. Thus, CD-NH2 and acetone would be a stronger H-bond donor/H-bond acceptor pair than CD-NH2 and TATP, and the detection mechanism cannot be formulated as a simple displacement of H-bonded acetone molecules on the surface of CDs by TATP acting as a H-bonding competitor.

In summary, when TATP is added to the APT-CDs solution whose fluorescence was quenched in the presence of acetone, a certain part of the fluorescence is recovered as a result of the molecular aggregates formed. This recovered fluorescence arises from the noncovalent interactions of TATP molecules added to the medium with acetone molecules that have interacted with the carbon dots at the ground-state via H-bonding; TATP supramolecular aggregates formation disrupts the H-bonding interaction that plays a supporting role in fluorescence recovery, but these molecular aggregates do not affect the dynamic-mechanism. Therefore, only a certain ratio of the fluorescence quenched in the presence of acetone is recovered. This application of the interaction mechanism based on the formation of molecular aggregates, as summarized in Scheme 1 , to sensitive TATP assay has brought a rational and innovative approach to the direct determination of intact TATP, introduced to the literature for the first time.

The applicability of the proposed method was investigated in the presence of potentially interferent metal cations and anions commonly found in soil and water, and of camouflage materials (e.g., carried by passengers as personal items in their hand luggage). The recorded emission spectra (Fig. 5 a) and emission intensities at 455 nm wavelength (Fig. 5b) of APT-CDs and (APT-CDs+TATP) solutions prepared in the presence of possible interfering species were compared. The histogram (Fig. 5b), digital photographs taken under UV-lamp (Fig. 5c and 5d), and recovery values depicted in Table S3 show that possible interfering substances have negligible effects on the sensor material and TATP determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery of TATP from soil sample
ce:section.ce:para: Reference standard sandy soil samples were used as real samples to investigate the recoverability of TATP with the proposed method. Three different sandy soil samples were contaminated with TATP (in acetone) solution prepared at different concentrations. TATP solutions extracted from soil with acetone were analyzed with APT-CDs and recovery values ​​were calculated for each TATP concentration (Table 1 ). As a result of the experiments carried out with N = 3 replicates, TATP was recovered from sandy soil samples with high values ​​between 99.16 % and 103.13 % with the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the spectrofluorometric method against the reference GC–MS technique
ce:section.ce:para:
The literature GC–MS method[25] was modified and applied for TATP analysis. The characteristic chromatogram and MS peak of TATP (observed at 43 m/z) are given in Fig. S11. TATP working solutions in the concentration range of 0.5 × 10–6 – 10.0 × 10–6 mol l -1 were analyzed by GC–MS and the linear calibration equation obtained using the peak area and micromolar concentration (C) values ​​is given below.
Peak

×

4
)
( r = 0.9993 )

The developed and reference GC–MS methodology was applied to TATP-contaminated sandy soil samples and statistical comparison between the results was performed with N = 3 replicate analyses. When Table 2 is examined, no significant difference is observed between the results. The method was validated against the GC–MS method using Student's t- and F-tests to compare population means and variances, respectively. The confidence level used in validating the analytical results for TATP-contaminated soil samples was 95 % for Student's t- and F-tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: On-site determination of TATP by smartphone
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to demonstrate the on-site applicability of the developed method, the fluorescence color intensities of the prepared APT-CDs solutions with and without TATP were measured with the help of a smartphone application. The prepared solutions were placed in a dark box as summarized in Fig. 6 a and measurements were performed with the smartphone application (measurements were performed with the “colorimeter” application using a Samsung Galaxy A54 smartphone with an Android operating system). The spectra obtained from the program are given in Fig. 6b and the intensity values ​​increase depending on the TATP concentration at the 525 nm wavelength (Fig. 6c). Digital photographs of the prepared solutions and the color images obtained from the application are given in Fig. 6d The linear equation observed as intensity versus TATP concentration is as given below.

)
where I represents the fluorescence color intensity of TATP interacting with APT-CDs, and C represents the final nanomolar (nmol l -1) concentration of TATP. The limit of detection (LOD) for TATP was calculated as 0.75 nmol l -1 (LOD = 3σbl/m, σbl standing for the standard deviation of the blank emission intensity at 525 nm, and m denoting the slope of the linear portion of the calibration curve), and the coefficients of variation were 1.44 % (within-day) and 1.98 % (between-day).

In the developed method, sensitive and selective fluorometric determination of intact-TATP was carried out with carbon dot-based fluorescence nanoprobe (APT-CDs). This study brings several novelties to intact TATP detection, namely: (i) a direct determination is accomplished, although TATP is known not to be amenable to molecular spectroscopic (UV–Vis, IR, fluorescence, etc.) analysis due to the lack of electron-withdrawing or electron-donating groups; (ii) the designed technique is a rarely seen fluorescence turn-on method involving the partial recovery of statically and dynamically quenched fluorescence by the solvent; (iii) the mechanism of the assay is nano-aggregate formation of TATP involving acetone molecules associated with nitrogenated carbon dots, which is a first in the literature; (iv) the proposed system does not involve preliminary hydrolytic cleavage of TATP to H2O2, followed by hydrogen peroxide detection open to various interferences of peroxides and similar materials. It does not need H2O2, and can simply measure intact TATP. In fact, H2O2 is not an interferent either to our measurements. HMTD, the other important peroxide-explosive, gives a much weaker signal than that of TATP; (v) the system is robust, not affected from common interferents or most environmental conditions. The molecular aggregates observed in the presence of TATP are not formed with other explosives, common ions, and camouflage materials used to disguise explosives. Synthesis of fluorescent material APT-CDs was carried out by microwave-assisted method using L-ascorbic acid (AA), polyethyleneimine (PEI), and triethylenetetramine (TETA) molecular precursors. APT-CDs with 66.8 % quantum yield have excitation and emission wavelengths of 355 nm and 440 nm, respectively. Blue fluorescence of APT-CDs, whose microscopic and morphological characterizations were performed by various instrumental techniques (STEM, DLS, IR, Raman, XPS, and XRD), is quenched in the presence of acetone. The fluorescence-quenching mechanism observed in the presence of acetone is H-bonding supported PET. A significant portion of the fluorescence of APT-CDs is recovered by adding intact-TATP to the fluorescence-quenched APT-CDs solution in the critical concentration range. This is because acetone molecules interacting with APT-CDs via H-bonding in the ground state form molecular aggregates with TATP as a result of noncovalent interactions. This interaction of APT-CDs with acetone is interrupted by TATP aggregates, partly recovering the initial fluorescence. The formation of molecular aggregates was confirmed by both STEM-imaging and DLS-histogram. The “turn-on” fluorescence signals obtained in the presence of TATP with this innovative mechanism were monitored both spectrophotometrically and with a smartphone-application. The TATP detection limit in the developed method is 0.18 nmol l -1 for spectrophotometric measurements and 0.75 nmol l -1 for smartphone-application measurements. In addition, the molecular aggregates observed in the presence of TATP are not formed with other explosives (e.g., HMTD, NTO, TNT, TETRYL, RDX, HMX, TNP, and PETN) and TATP-hydrolyzate (H2O2). It was proven by the high recovery values (94.2 %–105.7 %) ​​that common metal cations (e.g., Fe2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, and Mg2+), anions (e.g., Cl-, NO3 -, NO2 -, SO4 2-, and CO3 2-), and camouflage materials (e.g., glucose, detergent, aspartame, paracetamol, and acetyl salicylic acid), which are considered as possible interferents in the selective TATP determination, do not have a significant effect. TATP extracted from contaminated sandy soil samples was analyzed with both APT-CDs and the reference GC–MS method without any significant difference between the obtained results at 95 % confidence level. The innovative mechanism of the developed method, the direct determination of intact-TATP without having to degrade TATP samples by preliminary acid hydrolysis, the limited response of the structural analog cyclic peroxide HMTD, and the ability to perform TATP analysis on-site with a smartphone-application are expected to enable APT-CDs to stand-out from existing methods in the literature and be an effective tool for public safety and security through more reliable analytical methodology.
